text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 14 October 2024 

Dr Patel
South Quay Medical Centre

12 Fisherman’s Walk, London, E14 5HQ
Dear Dr Patel
Michael Turner, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: H728319 NHS No. 8475639201
Flat 4, 22 Millwright Street, London, E14 3DR 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since his last appointment with our service earlier this year, he reports a clear deterioration in day-to-day control. He works in a care setting with irregular breaks and frequent patient transfers; he describes cumulative fatigue over successive shifts with a perceived worsening of events on late and back-to-back duties. Prior to this decline, the seizure frequency was not reliably quantified and episodes were described as possibly syncopal versus epileptic—uncertain. Over the past month he has kept a contemporaneous diary: Seizure days: 9/30 this month. He notes clustering after three consecutive long shifts, and occasional brief recoveries during periods of adequate rest.

He remains on his longstanding regimen without recent dose changes. He reports intermittent pre-event light-headedness and visual blurring on some days, while on others events are without warning. Post-event confusion is variably present, lasting minutes to an hour. There have been no witnessed tongue bites or clear incontinence. He denies new head injury. He has not been driving. He feels more anxious about unpredictability at work and has discussed informal rota adjustments with his manager, though these have been inconsistent. There have been two near-falls during patient transfers in the last fortnight; no injuries occurred. Sleep is fragmented on shifts, averaging 4–5 hours, improving to 7 hours on rest days. He reports no recent intercurrent illness, alcohol use is minimal, and hydration can be suboptimal on busy shifts.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with stable observations. Cardiovascular and neurological examinations were unremarkable. Weight and BMI are unchanged. There are no new focal deficits. We reviewed his diary and smartphone event log together, which corroborate the increased symptom burden and functional impact over the last month.

In summary, there is objective worsening based on his monthly diary showing Seizure days: 9/30 this month, in the context of occupational fatigue and irregular breaks. The earlier history suggested episodes possibly syncopal versus epileptic—uncertain; however, the current pattern indicates deterioration in frequency and impact, with safety concerns in a manual-handling environment. We discussed strategies to improve sleep opportunity, hydration, and regular meal breaks at work, and he will continue to log events with timing, precipitating factors, witness accounts, and recovery features. I have arranged an expedited review with our epilepsy nurse specialist for education around event documentation and workplace risk assessment. I will also request updated routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC) and an ECG via primary care to exclude reversible contributors, and we will consider further investigations depending on trends over the next 4–6 weeks. 

I will review him again in clinic in approximately eight weeks, sooner if there is further deterioration or any injury. He understands to seek urgent care if events escalate in duration, cluster without recovery, or if new neurological deficits occur.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides an explicit quantified statement: "Seizure days: 9/30 this month." This indicates seizures occurred on 9 days within a 30-day month. No other competing frequency is given that is more frequent. Therefore, normalize to '9 per month'. Calculation: 9 seizure days over approximately one month → 9 per month.
